summary: Meena Boppana reflects on her journey from passionate math enthusiast to questioning why she and nearly all her female friends with strong math backgrounds didn't major in math at Harvard. She co-directed a survey revealing that women aren't just entering Harvard with less math preparation—they're actively dropping out during their college years, with female math majors writing theses and pursuing graduate school at far lower rates than men. The survey found cultural issues at the core: women feel uncomfortable in math department spaces and unwelcome despite wanting to participate. In response, she founded Gender Inclusivity in Math (GIIM) to build community among women in math while engaging male allies in addressing systemic barriers.
key_points: The gender gap in Harvard math is not primarily a 'pipeline issue' from high school—women are leaving math during their college years at disproportionate rates
Zero tenured women faculty exist in Harvard's math department, and only two women graduated with a primary math concentration
Survey data shows women feel uncomfortable in math department spaces and want to be involved but aren't
Breaking the silence about the gender gap revealed widespread concern among students of all genders who felt disempowered rather than apathetic
Male allies at student and faculty levels have shown strong support for change, suggesting institutional will exists
money_quotes: I think about how it is that virtually all my female friends with insanely strong math backgrounds (e.g. math competition stars) decided not to major in math
There are also a total of zero tenured women faculty in Harvard math
The main finding of our survey analysis is that the dearth of women in Harvard math is far more than a 'pipeline issue' stemming from high school
Women are dropping out of math during their years at Harvard
I had always assumed that because no one was talking about the gender gap, no one cared
I realized that my classmates of all genders feel more disempowered than apathetic
Mathematics is too beautiful and important to lose 50 percent (or much more when considering racial and class-based inequities) of the potential population of math lovers
key_insight: The absence of women in elite math programs stems not from inadequate preparation but from a hostile culture that pushes qualified women out during their studies.
